skill.name: docx-toolkit
skill.description: Create, edit, preview, validate, and format Word DOCX files with OpenXML-based command line tools and reference guides.
skill.summary: Build and maintain professional DOCX documents with OpenXML workflows.
skill.author: Fokkyp

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but most inspected hits are false positives from OpenXML namespaces, XML examples, and documentation snippets. Real risk remains because setup and helper scripts install dependencies, restore NuGet packages, and invoke external document parsers on local files.

medium_findings.title: Installer Scripts Use Elevated Package Installation
medium_findings.description: The setup scripts can run package managers with sudo or Windows package managers to install .NET, pandoc, and LibreOffice. This is legitimate setup behavior, but it can modify the host system and should require user consent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit dependency installation steps, not hidden behavior. Confidence is high that the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Remote Dependency Download And NuGet Restore
medium_findings.description: The Linux setup downloads the Microsoft dotnet-install script into /tmp and executes it. Both setup flows also restore NuGet packages, creating a supply-chain and network dependency risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network activity is visible and tied to documented dependency setup. It still depends on remote package sources and should be disclosed.

medium_findings.title: External Document Parsers Process User Files
medium_findings.description: Helper scripts pass user-selected documents to LibreOffice, pandoc, and unzip. Arguments are quoted, reducing shell injection risk, but malformed documents still reach complex external parsers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are expected for DOCX workflows and use quoted arguments. The remaining risk is parser exposure from untrusted document inputs.

low_findings.title: OpenXML Namespace URLs Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings in XML and C# constants are OpenXML namespace identifiers and relationship type strings. I found no evidence that these strings trigger outbound network calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected locations define XML namespaces and content types. These are static identifiers used by OpenXML documents.

low_findings.title: Documentation And Sample Keyword Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Many weak-crypto, C2, path traversal, and sensitive-file alerts are caused by prose, XML examples, or sample document text. No evidence found that these lines implement malware behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review showed ordinary sample content and OpenXML markup examples. The static labels do not match the semantic context.

low_findings.title: Command Examples In Skill Documentation Are Expected
low_findings.description: Static command-substitution hits in documentation reflect intended command examples for building, validating, and previewing DOCX files. They are not hidden execution paths by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible user-facing workflow instructions. The risk depends on user execution and is covered by the medium script findings.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: I searched for common prompt injection phrases and claims of special authority. No evidence found in the reviewed skill files that attempts to override the audit process.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only matching phrase reviewed was a benign numbering comment about starting a fresh list. The skill description is assertive routing text, not an audit override.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sudo And Package Manager Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: Setup scripts use sudo and package managers to install system dependencies. This pattern can alter host state and should run only after user review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The elevated commands are direct and visible. They are legitimate setup steps but remain privileged operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup script downloads dotnet-install.sh to /tmp and runs it. This is a common installer flow, but it executes remotely sourced code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly downloads and executes an installer. The URL is an official dot.net endpoint, which lowers but does not remove risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Parser Invocation On Document Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper scripts call LibreOffice, pandoc, and unzip against user-supplied document paths. This broadens the trust boundary to third-party parsers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Quoted arguments reduce shell injection risk. Parser exposure remains relevant for untrusted DOC, DOCX, or archive inputs.

content.value_statement: Professional Word documents are hard to create reliably with raw XML or manual formatting. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through OpenXML workflows for creating, editing, validating, and formatting DOCX files.
actual_capabilities: Create new DOCX reports, letters, memos, and academic documents.
Edit existing DOCX files by replacing text, filling placeholders, and updating sections.
Apply style templates and validate output with XSD and business rules.
Preview DOCX content as plain text before making structural changes.
Convert legacy DOC files to DOCX when LibreOffice is available.
Provide OpenXML reference guidance for tables, images, headers, footers, and track changes.
limitations: Requires a local .NET SDK and NuGet package restore for the CLI tools.
DOC conversion depends on LibreOffice, which may not be installed.
Complex template merges may need custom C# work beyond simple CLI commands.
It does not guarantee legal, academic, or brand compliance without user review.

use_cases.title: Generate Client Reports
use_cases.description: Create structured DOCX reports with headings, page setup, tables, headers, and validation checks.

use_cases.title: Update Existing Forms
use_cases.description: Fill placeholders, replace outdated text, and preserve document structure in existing Word files.

use_cases.title: Apply Academic Templates
use_cases.description: Reformat thesis, paper, or manuscript drafts to match required DOCX template styles.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a DOCX report titled [title]. Include an executive summary, three sections, and a simple conclusion.

prompt_templates.title: Fill an Existing Template
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the attached DOCX file. Replace the placeholders with the supplied details and keep the original formatting.

prompt_templates.title: Apply a Style Template
prompt_templates.prompt: Reformat the source DOCX to match the attached template. Preserve all content and validate the final document.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Complex Document
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a multi-section DOCX with cover page, table of contents, headers, footers, tables, figures, and validation checks.

output_examples.input: A request to draft a business proposal as a Word document.
output_examples.output: A formatted DOCX proposal with title page, sections, tables, footer, page numbers, and validation notes.

output_examples.input: A DOCX contract template with missing client fields.
output_examples.output: A completed DOCX file with placeholders filled, original formatting preserved, and a short change summary.

output_examples.input: A thesis draft and a university formatting template.
output_examples.output: A reformatted thesis DOCX with matched styles, section breaks, headers, footers, and validation results.

best_practices: Run the environment check before the first document operation in each session.
Preview and analyze existing DOCX files before changing content or formatting.
Validate every generated or reformatted DOCX before delivery.
anti_patterns: Do not run setup scripts without reviewing the dependency installation steps.
Do not edit raw DOCX XML when the CLI or OpenXML SDK can handle the structure.
Do not apply templates without checking that content, headings, and section breaks were preserved.

faq.question: Does this skill create real DOCX files?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses OpenXML-based tools to create and modify Word DOCX files locally.

faq.question: Can it edit an existing Word document?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports previewing, analyzing, replacing text, filling placeholders, and updating sections.

faq.question: Can it apply a company or university template?
faq.answer: Yes. It can apply template formatting and run validation checks after the change.

faq.question: What software is required?
faq.answer: The core tools require .NET. Pandoc and LibreOffice are optional for preview and DOC conversion.

faq.question: Is it safe to run on sensitive documents?
faq.answer: It works locally, but users should review scripts and avoid untrusted documents with external parsers.

faq.question: Does it replace Microsoft Word?
faq.answer: No. It automates DOCX structure and formatting, but final visual review in Word is still recommended.
